title: Kidneys — filtering blood and making urine
examTip: The kidney is a classic AQA target. Expect questions on what is and isn't filtered, what is reabsorbed, what makes urine more or less concentrated, and the treatment of kidney failure. Know the difference between dialysis and transplant, and be able to link kidney function to water balance (osmoregulation).
introTips: The kidney's job is to clean your blood — filtering everything out and then carefully putting back what you need.
AQA loves asking about what is filtered vs. what is reabsorbed — glucose is always fully reabsorbed (you need it), urea is not (it's waste).
Kidney failure treatment (dialysis vs. transplant) is a frequent 6-mark question target — know the pros and cons of each.
intro: Every hour, your kidneys filter about 7.5 litres of blood. They remove waste products (especially urea from protein breakdown), regulate water levels, and return useful substances to the blood. Without kidneys, urea would accumulate to toxic levels within days. Understanding how they filter, what they reabsorb, and what treatments exist when they fail is essential B5 knowledge.

keyPoints.heading: The kidney's role — filtration and excretion
keyPoints.content: The kidneys perform two main jobs. First, excretion: removing waste products — particularly urea (produced in the liver when excess amino acids are broken down), excess water, and excess ions. Second, osmoregulation: regulating the water content of the blood. Urea is the main nitrogenous waste product. The kidneys filter the blood to remove urea, producing urine, which travels down the ureter to the bladder and is excreted via the urethra. The concentration of urine varies depending on how much water needs to be conserved.
writeBullets: Kidneys excrete: urea (from amino acid breakdown in liver), excess water, excess ions
Urea is the main nitrogenous waste — toxic if it accumulates
Kidneys regulate water content of blood (osmoregulation)
Urine produced → ureter → bladder → urethra → excreted
Urine concentration varies depending on hydration and ADH levels

keyTerms.def: A waste product produced in the liver when excess amino acids are broken down; excreted by the kidneys in urine
The regulation of water and ion concentration in the blood and body fluids, carried out by the kidneys
The removal of metabolic waste products from the body

keyPoints.memTip: The three things kidneys remove: Urea, excess Water, excess Ions — UWI. Urea comes from protein breakdown in the liver (not made in the kidneys — just filtered there). Osmoregulation = regulating water, not removing all of it.

keyPoints.heading: Filtration and selective reabsorption in the nephron
keyPoints.content: The functional unit of the kidney is the nephron. Blood is forced under high pressure into a cup-shaped structure called the Bowman's capsule, where small molecules are filtered out of the blood (a process called ultrafiltration). Large molecules like proteins and blood cells are too big to filter through — they stay in the blood. The filtrate contains: water, glucose, urea, and mineral ions. As the filtrate passes along the tubule, the body selectively reabsorbs useful substances back into the blood. All glucose is reabsorbed (via active transport). Most water is reabsorbed. Some ions are reabsorbed. Urea remains in the filtrate and becomes part of urine.
writeBullets: Nephron = functional unit of the kidney
Ultrafiltration: high pressure forces small molecules out of blood into Bowman's capsule
Proteins and blood cells are too large to filter — they stay in the blood
Filtrate contains: water, glucose, urea, mineral ions
Selective reabsorption: all glucose reabsorbed (active transport), most water reabsorbed, some ions reabsorbed
Urea is NOT reabsorbed — it remains in filtrate and forms urine

keyTerms.def: The functional unit of the kidney; carries out filtration and selective reabsorption
The process by which high blood pressure forces small molecules (water, glucose, urea, ions) out of the blood into the Bowman's capsule
The process by which useful substances (especially glucose and water) are reabsorbed from the filtrate back into the bloodstream
The cup-shaped structure at the start of a nephron where ultrafiltration takes place

keyPoints.memTip: What stays in the blood? Big things — proteins and blood cells (too large to filter). What is always reabsorbed? Glucose — you never want to lose glucose. What is never reabsorbed? Urea — it's waste. What varies? Water — depends on how dehydrated you are.

keyPoints.heading: ADH and water reabsorption
keyPoints.content: ADH (Anti-Diuretic Hormone) is produced by the pituitary gland and controls how much water is reabsorbed by the kidneys. When the blood is too concentrated (e.g. you're dehydrated or have eaten salty food), the hypothalamus detects this, the pituitary releases more ADH, which causes the collecting duct of the nephron to become more permeable to water, so more water is reabsorbed back into the blood. This produces small amounts of concentrated urine. When the blood is too dilute (e.g. you've drunk a lot of water), less ADH is released, the collecting duct is less permeable, less water is reabsorbed, and you produce large amounts of dilute urine. This is negative feedback controlling water balance.
writeBullets: ADH (Anti-Diuretic Hormone): produced by the pituitary gland
ADH controls permeability of the collecting duct to water
Dehydrated / high blood concentration → more ADH → more water reabsorbed → small, concentrated urine
Over-hydrated / low blood concentration → less ADH → less water reabsorbed → large, dilute urine
Hypothalamus detects blood water concentration and triggers pituitary to release ADH

keyTerms.def: Anti-Diuretic Hormone; produced by the pituitary gland; increases permeability of the kidney collecting duct to water, increasing water reabsorption
A gland at the base of the brain that produces and releases ADH in response to signals from the hypothalamus
The final part of the nephron; its permeability to water is controlled by ADH, determining how concentrated the urine is

keyPoints.memTip: ADH = Anti-Diuretic. Diuresis means producing a lot of urine. Anti-diuretic means LESS urine (more water reabsorbed). More ADH → less urine. Less ADH → more urine. Dehydrated → need to conserve water → more ADH. Just had a litre of water → need to get rid of it → less ADH.

keyPoints.heading: Kidney failure — dialysis and transplant
keyPoints.content: If kidneys fail, waste products and excess water accumulate in the blood. Two treatments exist. Dialysis: blood is passed through a machine along a partially permeable membrane. The dialysis fluid on the other side has the same concentration of useful substances (glucose, ions) as normal blood, so only waste (urea) and excess ions diffuse out. Dialysis is needed 3× per week, for 3–4 hours each time, and is a lifelong commitment unless a transplant is received. Kidney transplant: a donor kidney (from a living or recently deceased donor) is surgically implanted. The patient needs to take immunosuppressant drugs for life to prevent rejection. Transplant is preferable long-term as it restores normal kidney function, but there is a shortage of donors and a risk of rejection.
writeBullets: Kidney failure: urea and excess water/ions accumulate in blood
Dialysis: blood filtered through partially permeable membrane next to dialysis fluid
Dialysis fluid: same glucose and ion concentration as normal blood → urea diffuses out
Dialysis needed 3× per week, 3–4 hours each session — significant lifestyle impact
Transplant: donor kidney implanted; immunosuppressants taken lifelong to prevent rejection
Transplant advantage: restores normal function; disadvantage: donor shortage, rejection risk

keyTerms.def: A treatment for kidney failure where blood is filtered through a machine using a partially permeable membrane to remove waste products
Drugs taken after a kidney transplant to prevent the immune system from rejecting the new kidney
When the immune system identifies a transplanted organ as foreign and attacks it — prevented by tissue matching and immunosuppressants

keyPoints.memTip: Dialysis = machine does the kidney's job. Works by diffusion across a partially permeable membrane (just like osmosis in cells). The dialysis fluid is normal blood concentration — so only EXTRA stuff (urea, excess ions) diffuses out. Transplant = real kidney, but requires drugs for life and risks rejection.

tables.title: Dialysis vs kidney transplant
rows: Machine filters blood via partially permeable membrane
Access issues, infection at access point
Limited by donor shortage; long waiting lists

0.question: Which substances are removed from the blood by the kidneys?
options: Urea, excess water and excess ions
Urea, glucose and red blood cells
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — urea (the main waste product), excess water, and excess ions are what the kidneys remove. Glucose is filtered but always reabsorbed.
0.wrongFeedback: The kidneys excrete urea (waste from amino acid breakdown), excess water, and excess ions. They don't permanently remove glucose — glucose is filtered out but then selectively reabsorbed. Proteins are too large to filter. Carbon dioxide is removed by the lungs, not the kidneys.

2.question: A person is dehydrated. What happens to their ADH level and urine production?
options: ADH decreases; large amounts of dilute urine produced
ADH increases; small amounts of concentrated urine produced
ADH decreases; small amounts of concentrated urine produced
ADH stays the same; urine production is unaffected
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — dehydration means blood concentration is too high, so more ADH is released to conserve water, making the urine small in volume and concentrated.
2.wrongFeedback: When dehydrated, the blood is too concentrated. The hypothalamus detects this and the pituitary releases MORE ADH. More ADH makes the collecting duct more permeable to water → more water is reabsorbed → small amounts of concentrated urine is produced. It's the anti-diuretic hormone — more of it = less urine output.

3.question: Why does a kidney transplant patient need to take immunosuppressant drugs?
options: To prevent the new kidney from producing too much urine
To prevent the immune system from attacking and rejecting the donor kidney
To increase urea production in the new kidney
To reduce the risk of dialysis being needed after the transplant
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — the immune system recognises the donor kidney as foreign tissue and would attack it. Immunosuppressants prevent this rejection.
3.wrongFeedback: Immunosuppressants are needed to suppress the immune system's natural response of attacking the donor kidney as 'foreign' tissue. Without them, the immune system would reject the transplant. The patient needs these drugs for life, which also makes them more vulnerable to infections.

commonMistakes: Saying glucose is excreted in urine — it isn't normally. All glucose is reabsorbed from the filtrate. Glucose in urine is a sign of uncontrolled diabetes, not normal kidney function.
Saying proteins are filtered out of the blood — proteins are too large to pass through the filter at Bowman's capsule. Protein in urine indicates kidney damage.

Confusing ADH direction — more ADH = more water reabsorbed = LESS urine (concentrated). Less ADH = less water reabsorbed = MORE urine (dilute). The anti-diuretic hormone does the opposite of what it sounds like it might do.
Forgetting that dialysis works by diffusion — the dialysis fluid has the same concentration of useful substances as blood, so only excess substances (urea, extra ions) diffuse across the membrane.
Thinking a kidney transplant automatically solves all problems — the patient still needs immunosuppressants for life, which suppress the immune system and increase infection risk.
examTips: When asked what is filtered but not reabsorbed, the answer is urea (and some excess ions). When asked what is filtered AND reabsorbed, the answer is glucose (all of it) and most water.
For dialysis questions, explain the concentration gradient: dialysis fluid is at normal blood concentration for useful substances, so only waste products (urea) have a gradient to diffuse out.

ADH questions often involve a graph or scenario. Always link: blood concentration up → more ADH → more water reabsorbed → concentrated urine. Blood concentration down → less ADH → less water reabsorbed → dilute urine.

Evaluate questions on dialysis vs transplant usually expect: transplant is better quality of life and restores normal function, BUT requires a matching donor (shortage), risk of rejection, and lifelong immunosuppressants.
revisionCardBullets: Kidneys excrete: urea, excess water, excess ions
Urea = waste from amino acid breakdown in the liver
Nephron = functional unit of kidney; filtration + selective reabsorption
Ultrafiltration: small molecules filtered into Bowman's capsule; proteins/blood cells stay in blood
Selective reabsorption: all glucose, most water, some ions reabsorbed back into blood
Urea is NOT reabsorbed — remains in filtrate → forms urine
ADH (from pituitary): controls water reabsorption via collecting duct permeability
Dehydrated → more ADH → more water reabsorbed → small, concentrated urine
Dialysis: machine filters blood via partially permeable membrane, urea diffuses out
Transplant: donor kidney; requires immunosuppressants for life to prevent rejection
